To find the probability that a student from Group B has higher engagement than a student from Group A we define a new variable D as X_B minus X_A. Since both X_A and X_B are normally distributed D is also normally distributed with a mean of 75 minus 70 which equals 5 and a variance of 12 squared plus 10 squared which equals 244. The standard deviation of D is the square root of 244 or approximately 15.62. We need the probability that D is greater than 0. We calculate the z-score as 0 minus 5 divided by 15.62 which gives approximately -0.32. Using the standard normal table the area to the left of -0.32 is about 0.3745 so the area to the right is 1 minus 0.3745 or 0.6255. This means there is roughly a 62.55 percent chance that a randomly selected student from Group B has higher engagement.

For the performance improvement part we set the exponential function equal to the logistic function to find the time t. The equation is 50 times e to the power of 0.02t equals 100 divided by 1 plus e to the power of negative 0.15 times t minus 5. This is a complex transcendental equation that requires numerical methods for an exact solution. An explanation of the process shows that we must iterate to find where the curves intersect. Nothing forces an algebraic closed form solution here. By testing values we find that at t equals 10 the exponential value is about 61.07 and the logistic value is about 73.11. At t equals 15 the exponential value is about 69.35 and the logistic value is about 88.08. At t equals 20 the exponential value is about 79.10 and the logistic value is about 95.26. The curves do not seem to cross in the early stages because the logistic growth starts slower but accelerates. Wait let us recheck the logistic function behavior at t=5.
